text: RL promise is credible only for specific TE tasks. The wording must not generalize from critical-flow rerouting to all network-flow optimization.
Keeping solvers as default is right for offline planning, but online control sometimes needs second-scale or subsecond decisions where exact optimization is too slow.
Show performance on unseen traffic matrices, topology changes, and failure cases. Otherwise the simulator may be teaching the policy the benchmark rather than the network.
Safety constraints are binding, but a shielded RL selector could still be worthwhile if it measurably reduces solve time or congestion in high-frequency decisions.
The repeated adoption pattern is hybridization: learned components enter around trusted optimizers before anyone allows full control-plane replacement.
Learning-accelerated optimization aligns the research evidence with operational safety better than end-to-end RL.
Shadow mode and fallback are non-negotiable for any network routing policy that can affect customer traffic.
Economic upside is plausible only after the organization proves traffic volatility, value per decision, and no degradation under failures.
Benchmark instrumentation is the first shared investment because it helps both solver tuning and RL evaluation.
Start advisory or bounded. Production control should follow evidence, not novelty.
